eli3: Cross-entropy measures the 'distance' between two probability distributions. It's H(p,q) = -Σ p(x) log q(x). Think of it as: if the true distribution is p, how surprised are you on average if you believe distribution q?
Cross-entropy combines two things: the inherent uncertainty of p (its entropy) plus the 'penalty' for using q instead of p (KL divergence). So H(p,q) = H(p) + KL(p||q).
In classification, we minimize cross-entropy loss between true labels (one-hot p) and predicted probabilities (softmax q). This forces predictions to match the true distribution.

whenWhy.title: Classification loss functions
whenWhy.body: Cross-entropy loss (log loss) is standard for multi-class classification: L = -Σ yᵢ log(ŷᵢ). Penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily. Outperforms MSE for classification because it matches probability interpretation and has better gradients.

whenWhy.title: Language modeling
whenWhy.body: Perplexity = exp(cross-entropy). Measures how well a language model predicts next words. Lower cross-entropy = lower perplexity = better model. Used to evaluate GPT, BERT, and all language models.

whenWhy.title: Generative models
whenWhy.body: VAEs use reconstruction loss (often MSE or cross-entropy) plus KL regularization. GANs implicitly minimize Jensen-Shannon divergence (symmetrized KL). Diffusion models use cross-entropy-like objectives for noise prediction.

whenWhy.title: Multi-label and hierarchical classification
whenWhy.body: Binary cross-entropy for each label independently. Sigmoid + BCE instead of softmax + CE. Handles multiple positive labels. Hierarchical cross-entropy exploits class hierarchies for better generalization.

deepDiveIntro: Cross-entropy between two distributions measures the average surprise when you assume distribution q but the true distribution is p. It equals the entropy of the true distribution plus the KL divergence from p to q. Because the entropy of p is a constant (it doesn't depend on your model q), minimising cross-entropy is exactly equivalent to minimising KL divergence — which means pushing your predicted distribution as close to the true one as possible.

In classification, the true distribution is a one-hot vector (probability 1 on the correct class, 0 elsewhere). Cross-entropy then reduces to just the negative log of the predicted probability assigned to the correct class. Minimising this is the same as maximising the likelihood of the training labels under your model — cross-entropy loss and maximum likelihood estimation are the same thing.

deepDive.heading: Definition and Relation to KL
deepDive.body: Cross-entropy is the average log-probability assigned to true outcomes by your model, negated. It decomposes into the irreducible entropy of the true distribution plus the KL divergence — the extra penalty for using the wrong distribution. Since the true distribution's entropy is fixed, the only way to reduce cross-entropy is to reduce the KL divergence, meaning your predictions must match the true distribution more closely.
deepDive.formula: H(p,q) = H(p) + D(p||q) = -Σ p(x) log q(x)

deepDive.heading: Binary Cross-Entropy
deepDive.body: For binary classification (yes/no outcomes), the loss penalises confident wrong predictions heavily and barely penalises confident correct predictions. If the true label is 1 and the model outputs probability 0.01, the loss is enormous; if the model outputs 0.99, the loss is tiny. This asymmetry gives strong gradient signals when the model is confidently wrong, enabling fast learning. For multi-label problems, binary cross-entropy is applied independently to each label.

deepDive.heading: Categorical Cross-Entropy
deepDive.body: For multi-class classification, the loss simplifies to just the negative log of the probability assigned to the correct class — all the other classes drop out because their one-hot targets are zero. If the model is confident and correct the loss is near zero; if the model confidently picks the wrong class the loss approaches infinity. For numerical stability, always compute the softmax and log together by subtracting the maximum logit first before exponentiating.

deepDive.heading: Label Smoothing
deepDive.body: Instead of training the model to output probability 1 for the correct class and 0 for everything else, label smoothing assigns a small probability (typically 0.1 divided across all other classes) to the wrong classes. This prevents the model from becoming overconfident, acts as a regulariser, and tends to improve calibration — the model's stated confidence more accurately reflects its actual accuracy. Label smoothing is standard in training modern transformers and image classifiers.
deepDive.formula: y'_k = 1 - ε if k = true, ε/(K-1) otherwise

deepDive.heading: Focal Loss
deepDive.body: In object detection, the vast majority of image regions contain background (easy negatives) and only a few contain actual objects. Standard cross-entropy gets overwhelmed by the easy background examples and barely updates on the rare, hard foreground examples. Focal loss multiplies the standard cross-entropy by a factor that down-weights examples the model already classifies confidently, directing gradient signal toward the hard cases. The parameter gamma controls how aggressively easy examples are suppressed.

takeaways: H(p,q) = H(p) + D(p||q). Minimizing CE = minimizing KL.
Classification: CE = -log(predicted probability of true class).
Binary CE for multi-label; softmax CE for multi-class.
Label smoothing and focal loss are CE variants for regularization and imbalance.

edgeCases.q: What happens when q(x) = 0 but p(x) > 0?
edgeCases.a: Cross-entropy is infinite (∞). This means the model assigns zero probability to a true outcome, which is catastrophic. In practice, we clip probabilities (e.g., ε = 1e-7) to avoid numerical issues. This is why we don't use hard 0/1 targets in training.

edgeCases.q: Why is cross-entropy better than MSE for classification?
edgeCases.a: (1) Gradients: CE has steeper gradients when predictions are wrong, faster learning. (2) Probabilistic: CE is negative log-likelihood, MSE assumes Gaussian errors. (3) Saturation: sigmoid + MSE gradients vanish when saturated; CE gradients don't. (4) Convexity: CE loss is convex in logits; MSE isn't.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between cross-entropy and log loss?
edgeCases.a: They're the same thing. 'Log loss' is common in competitions (Kaggle) and scikit-learn. 'Cross-entropy' is common in deep learning frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow). Both refer to -Σ p(x) log q(x). Binary log loss = binary cross-entropy.

sources.label: Goodfellow et al. — Deep Learning, Ch. 6
Lin et al. — Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection
